intro:
Bethel (Hebrew Beth-el, "house of God") was a city in the central highlands about twelve miles north of Jerusalem, on the watershed ridge road at the border of Benjamin and Ephraim. Originally called Luz, it became, after Jerusalem, the most frequently named town in the Old Testament — and one of the most theologically charged, for its story runs from the patriarchs' meeting with God to the depths of Israel's idolatry, embodying the whole tension between true and false worship.

Bethel first appears when Abram pitched his tent and built an altar between Bethel and Ai and "called upon the name of the LORD" (Genesis 12:8). But it is forever bound to Jacob, who, fleeing from Esau, lay down at Luz and dreamed of a stairway set up to heaven with the angels of God ascending and descending, while the LORD stood above and renewed the Abrahamic promise. Waking in awe — "Surely the LORD is in this place... this is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven" — he set up his stone as a pillar, anointed it, named the place Bethel, and vowed it would be God's house (Genesis 28:10–22). Years later God called Jacob back: he purged his household of foreign gods, built an altar he named El-Bethel, and there God confirmed his new name, Israel (Genesis 35:1–15).

In the days of the judges Bethel was a place to seek God, where the ark of the covenant rested for a time and Israel inquired of the LORD (Judges 20:26–27), and Samuel included it on his yearly circuit of judgment (1 Samuel 7:16). Its trajectory darkened irreversibly after the kingdom divided, when Jeroboam I set up one of his two golden calves there with a counterfeit priesthood and festival, declaring, "Behold your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt" (1 Kings 12:28–33). This became "the sin of Jeroboam" that defined the northern kingdom. A man of God from Judah cried out against the altar at Bethel, naming a future king, Josiah, who would defile it (1 Kings 13:1–2). The prophets aimed their sharpest oracles at it: Amos warned, "do not seek Bethel... for Bethel shall come to nothing" (Amos 5:5; Amos 4:4), and Hosea mocked it as Beth-aven, "house of wickedness" (Hosea 4:15).

The long-delayed word was fulfilled when King Josiah, in his great reform, pulled down the altar and high place at Bethel, burned it, and defiled it with human bones — sparing only the tomb of the prophet who had foretold it all (2 Kings 23:15–18). Bethel thus stands as a sober parable: the very "house of God," where heaven's gate once opened to a fugitive, became the shrine of the calf and the "house of wickedness," a warning that a sacred place without faithful worship invites judgment rather than blessing. The deepest fulfilment of Jacob's vision lies beyond the town itself, for Jesus took up its imagery to declare himself the true ladder between heaven and earth: "you will see heaven opened, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man" (John 1:51).
